rows.v: Lean cut from the back; chops and roasts. Easy to overcook.
Fatty underside; bacon, braises, and crisp-skin roasts.
Butt/Boston butt; collagen-rich, ideal for slow pulled pork.
The leanest, most tender muscle; quick-cooking.
From the belly side of the ribs; meatier, fattier than baby back.
From the loin near the spine; leaner, more curved, tender.
The hind leg, uncured; roasted as fresh ham.
Joint above the trotter; gelatinous, for braises and stock.
Rib section; the elegant Frenched roast, quick to cook.
Large hind cut; roasted bone-in or butterflied.
Tender mini T-bones from the loin.
Fattier, flavorful; braises and slow roasts.
Lower leg; collagen-rich, braised until falling off the bone.
Meat from sheep over two years; stronger, gamier flavor.
Hard-working, tough cut prized for stews and ragù.
Lean white meat; dries out fast, cook to 74°C/165°F.
Dark, forgiving, flavorful; the cook's favorite.
Roasted or spatchcocked; the Sunday centerpiece.
Drumette + flat; fried, roasted, or braised.
Lower leg, dark meat; cheap and juicy.
Offal; pâté, parfait, and seared dishes.
Large bird; lean breast vs richer leg, the holiday roast.
Rich, fatty-skinned; seared rare to medium (magret).
Tougher dark meat; the classic confit cut.
Dark, very fatty bird; festive roast, rendered fat prized.
Tiny, delicate bird; quick-roasted or grilled.
Young domestic pigeon; tender, dark, served pink.
Leaner, gamier than chicken; roasted whole.
Deer; very lean, dark, mineral; cook rare to avoid drying.
Lean white meat; braised or roasted, mild.
Leaner, gamier pork relative; braises well.
Darker, richer than rabbit; classic in jugged stews.
Lean, sweet, beef-like; cook gently, low fat.
Very lean large game; mild, tender when not overcooked.
Lean game bird; prone to drying, often barded or braised.
Small game bird; delicate, roasted quickly.
Strong, heathery wild flavor; the prized British game bird.
Leaner, gamier than farmed; mallard, teal, widgeon.
Tiny, intensely flavored wader; traditional roasted whole.
Young calf; pale, tender, mild; loin or rib chop.
Cross-cut shank for osso buco; marrow and gelatin.
Thymus/pancreas offal; creamy, delicate, prized.
Calf or pork liver; iron-rich, seared or for pâté.
Offal with mineral tang; steak-and-kidney, devilled.
Stomach lining; long-cooked in menudo, trippa, pho.
Braised then peeled; tender, rich; tacos de lengua.
Gelatin-rich tail (often beef but classic cross-cut); deep braises.
Ground seasoned meat in casing; fresh, smoked, or cured.
Cured, often smoked pork belly or back.
Dry-cured ham, sliced paper-thin; Parma, San Daniele.
Salt-cured pork belly; Italian, unsmoked, for cooking.
Slow-cooked and preserved in fat; classic for duck.
Salt-water soak for moisture and seasoning, esp. poultry/pork.
Preserving with salt/nitrite; the basis of charcuterie.
Meat slow-cooked and shredded into a spreadable potted form.
